title: Firewall Rule Design
description: Best practices for writing effective firewall rules — default deny, least privilege, and rule ordering.
fullExplanation: Firewall rules define what traffic is allowed or denied. Rules are processed in order — the first matching rule wins. Good rule design follows the principle of least privilege: deny everything by default, then explicitly allow only what is needed. Rules should be specific (narrow source/destination/port), documented, and regularly reviewed. Overly permissive rules (allow any any) are a common misconfiguration.

principles.description: Block all traffic by default. Explicitly allow only what is needed. 'Implicit deny' at end of rule list.
Allow only the minimum necessary traffic. Specific source IPs, destination IPs, and ports.
More specific rules first. General rules last. First match wins.
Allow established/related traffic automatically — don't write rules for return traffic.
Filter outbound traffic too — prevent data exfiltration and C2 callbacks.
Every rule should have a comment explaining why it exists and who requested it.

commonRulePatterns.description: Permit return traffic for connections initiated from inside
HTTP/HTTPS to web servers, SMTP to mail servers — specific destination IPs
Drop packets from internet with private source IPs (anti-spoofing)
Last rule: deny everything not explicitly permitted

keyFacts: Rule bloat: hundreds of old, undocumented rules — review and clean up regularly
Shadow rules: rule never matches because earlier rule catches all traffic
Any/any rule: allow all traffic — common misconfiguration, defeats firewall purpose
Ingress filtering: block spoofed source IPs at network edge (BCP38)
Egress filtering: block outbound traffic to known malicious IPs/domains
Change management: all firewall changes should go through approval process
Firewall audit: tools like Tufin, AlgoSec analyze rule sets for conflicts and risks
